results.possibleIdentity: Cyril Stanley Smith (1903–1992) was a renowned metallurgist and historian of technology who was deeply involved in the intersection of art and technology during the late 1960s and 1970s. As a professor at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) and a key intellectual figure in the orbit of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), Smith championed the idea that the aesthetic curiosity of artists and artisans often precedes and inspires scientific and technological discovery. He was a frequent collaborator with the Center for Advanced Visual Studies (CAVS) at MIT, which shared common goals with E.A.T. in promoting collaborations between artists and scientists. Smith authored influential works such as "Art, Technology, and Science: Notes on Their Historical Interaction" (1970) and curated the Smithsonian exhibition "Aspects of Art and Science" in 1978. He is often cited in E.A.T. literature as a visionary whose theories provided a philosophical foundation for the group's collaborative projects.

Cyril M. Harris (also known as Cyril Harris) was a prominent acoustics engineer and professor of Electrical Engineering and Architecture at Columbia University who collaborated closely with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) in the late 1960s and early 1970s. Harris was one of the technical experts who frequently interacted with artists to explore new media. In 1968, he served as a juror for the E.A.T. international competition which selected engineering-driven artworks for the MoMA exhibition "The Machine as Seen at the End of the Mechanical Age." During the same year, he delivered a lecture-demonstration titled "Acoustics of Enclosed Spaces" as part of the E.A.T. lecture series held at their loft in New York City. His expertise in sound and acoustics was also utilized in the development of the E.A.T.-designed Pepsi Pavilion for the 1970 World Expo in Osaka, Japan.

Cyril Mann (1911–1980) was a British painter and sculptor who was active throughout the late 1960s and 1970s. Known for his intense exploration of light, shadow, and sunlight on surfaces in movement, he presented his work in numerous solo exhibitions and one-man shows during this period. While he was a contemporary of the Art and Technology movement and exhibited in the same era as E.A.T., his direct membership in the organization is not explicitly documented in E.A.T. archival summaries; however, his artistic focus on movement and light aligns with the interests of many E.A.T. collaborators of the time.

results.possibleIdentity: Bill Mullen was an experimental composer and violist active in the late 1960s. He was a member of the inaugural 1967 class of the University of California, San Diego (UCSD) Music Department, which was founded with a mission to integrate making and performing music with experimental studies, technology, and 20th-century theoretical concerns. Mullen was part of a cohort of experimental music pioneers that included figures like Jack Logan, Alan Strange, and Alan Johnson. The department's focus on the intersection of performance and technology aligns with the goals of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization.

Bill Mullen was a commercial artist and Art Director active in the late 1960s. In 1969, he served as the Art Director for high-profile advertising campaigns, such as Air France's "The armchair explorer's guide," working at the agency Kenyon & Eckhardt Inc. During this period, he collaborated with prominent visual artists like Milton Glaser and Seymour Chwast (founders of Push Pin Studios). Given the close relationship between the commercial art world and the avant-garde Art and Technology movement in New York during this era, he represents a possible identity for the artist described.

results.possibleIdentity: Norma Jean Squires (also Norma-Jean Squires) is an American artist and educator active from the early 1960s through the 21st century, primarily based in California, New Jersey, and Ontario. She graduated from The Cooper Union for the Advancement of Science and Art in 1961 (A'61). Squires is known for her work in sculpture and painting, often working in abstract styles with mediums such as acrylic, interference on wood panels, prismacolor, oilstick, and pastel. In 1975, she was featured in the "4x8+4x4" exhibition at the Newport Harbor Art Museum, which showcased young Southern California artists. She has been a member of the Southern California Women's Caucus for Art (SCWCA) and the artist collective Group Nine. Her work has been exhibited at the Orlando Gallery in Encino, California, and she participated in a 2008 traveling exhibition in Mexico sponsored by the General Consulate of Mexico in Los Angeles. Squires has been associated with the intersection of art and technology, participating in panels such as "Women Humanizing High Technology" at the Women's Caucus for Art conference. Her artistic themes have included explorations of the cosmos, spirituality, and eroticism, the latter through her long-term collaboration with artist Merrilyn Duzy on the project "The New Art: Female Eroticism."

results.possibleIdentity: Gary Williams (sometimes Gary J. Williams) is a theatre historian, scholar, and artist associated with the Catholic University of America (CUA). He is notably linked to the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) through archival records at the Smithsonian Institution, specifically within the Hirshhorn Museum and Sculpture Garden's exhibition records. His work, including 'Photographs of Production,' is documented in relation to major exhibitions such as 'Artistic Collaboration in the Twentieth Century' (1984) and 'Different Drummers' (1988), which explored the intersection of art and technology and the legacy of E.A.T. Williams was active as a researcher and likely a documentarian or collaborator in the late 1960s and 1970s. He later served as a Professor of Drama at the Catholic University of America from 1974 until his retirement as Professor Emeritus in 2002, where he also edited the academic journal 'Theatre Survey.'

Gary Williams is an American visual artist and muralist based in Cleveland, Ohio, known for his work in public art and community activism. He began his artistic journey in the 1960s, a period that informed his later socially conscious work. Alongside artist Robin Robinson, Williams is recognized for creating prominent murals such as 'Our Lives Matter' and 'Nubian Graces' in the Cleveland area. His career has spanned decades, and he currently serves on the Board of Directors for the Collective Arts Network (CAN). His work often focuses on themes of social justice, identity, and community empowerment, utilizing both traditional mural techniques and modern augmented reality technologies.

John Gary Williams (1946–2019) was an American R&B and soul singer, songwriter, and artist best known as the lead vocalist and founding member of the vocal group The Mad Lads. Active throughout the late 1960s and 1970s, he recorded for the legendary Stax Records (on the Volt sub-label). Following his service in the Vietnam War and a brief period of civil rights activism with the group 'The Invaders' in Memphis, he launched a solo career in the early 1970s, releasing a self-titled album in 1973 that is regarded as a cult classic in the soul genre. His career experienced a resurgence in the 2010s before his death in 2019.

results.possibleIdentity: Alex Katz (born July 24, 1927) is a pre-eminent American figurative artist known for his large-scale paintings, sculptures, and prints. Emerging in the 1950s, he became particularly active and influential during the late 1960s and 1970s, a period when he transitioned from small collages to monumentally scaled canvases and multi-panel works. His style is characterized by bold simplicity, flattened forms, and economy of line, often cited as a precursor to Pop Art. His most frequent subject is his wife and muse, Ada, whom he married in 1958. Katz is also known for his 'cutouts'—freestanding or wall-mounted portraits painted on shaped aluminum. Throughout the 1960s and 1970s, he was deeply involved in the New York intellectual and artistic scene, portraying a social world of poets, critics, and fellow artists. He collaborated extensively with the choreographer Paul Taylor starting in 1960, designing numerous sets and costumes. In the context of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), Katz is associated with the organization's legacy of cross-disciplinary collaboration; his work has been featured in retrospective exhibitions regarding E.A.T. (such as 'Sensing the Future' at Thaddaeus Ropac), and he is represented in collections alongside E.A.T. projects (such as at the Blanton Museum of Art). His son, Vincent Katz, has also written extensively on the collaborative spirit of the E.A.T. era.
